explanation: The statement expresses two concerns: (1) If either prop_0 or prop_1 holds, then ensure that prop_2 still holds. (2) If prop_3 holds and prop_4 never holds. For (1), we can express as: whenever prop_0 or prop_1 is true, G prop_2 should hold. For (2), it can be interpreted as: if prop_3 holds, G !prop_4, i.e., prop_4 is never true. Combined together, we have globally ((prop_0 | prop_1) -> G prop_2) & (prop_3 -> G !prop_4).
output_LTL: G ( (((returnCurrentPath & explainCurrentPath) | (returnIntendedPath & explainIntendedPath)) -> G (followPath)) & ((returnIntendedPath -> followPath) -> G !(!noDeviations)) )

explanation: The NL describes needing to know the intended path to verify adherence, and then gives a condition: 'if it would prop_0 and it won’t prop_1'. Interpreting this LTL-wise, we represent: 'if eventually prop_0 occurs, then globally prop_1 never occurs.' In LTL, that is: (F prop_0) -> G (!prop_1).

The sentence says: either (prop_0 or prop_1) is true so that prop_2 can be ensured, OR, alternatively, both prop_3 and prop_4 hold. Translated to LTL, this can be captured as: (if either prop_0 or prop_1 then prop_2), or if prop_3 and prop_4. Since the 'so I could make sure' implies a guarantee (globally), and the second part is a conjunction of prop_3 and prop_4.

The requirement expresses an interest in knowing about prop_0 or prop_1 to check if prop_2 still holds, or if prop_3 would occur and prop_4 would not occur. This is a disjunction of two situations: (1) whenever prop_0 or prop_1 hold, prop_2 holds; or (2) eventually, prop_3 occurs and prop_4 never does.

The specification describes two main options, connected by an 'Or': (1) Wanting either prop_0 or prop_1 as a way to achieve prop_2 (the intention is so that eventually prop_2 will be true if either prop_0 or prop_1 holds at some point), or (2) if prop_3 holds and prop_4 does not, regardless of the first option. The LTL formula expresses that either 'eventually (prop_0 or prop_1) and eventually prop_2', or 'eventually (prop_3 and not prop_4)'.

The requirement is satisfied if either (A) eventually, prop_0 or prop_1 holds and then prop_2 holds later; OR (B) both prop_3 and prop_4 hold at some point. The first part expresses that eventually you want to achieve either prop_0 or prop_1, and then (possibly later) prop_2. The second part allows satisfaction simply by both prop_3 and prop_4 happening.

The statement expresses two disjunctive desires: (1) that either prop_0 or prop_1 should happen, enabling prop_2 to occur, or (2) that if prop_3 occurs, then prop_4 should never occur. This translates to: (1) Eventually (prop_0 or prop_1) holds and eventually prop_2 holds, or (2) if eventually prop_3 occurs, then globally prop_4 does not occur.

The requirement asks to know whether the system is still following the intended path, or if it will eventually satisfy 'prop_0' but will never satisfy 'prop_1'. In LTL, this can be interpreted as globally, either 'prop_0' will eventually happen and 'prop_1' never happens, or the intended path is known to be followed. Since we do not have propositions for 'following path' or 'intended path,' the formal content is: If eventually 'prop_0' holds, then globally, 'prop_1' does not hold.

This requirement describes two alternative scenarios: (1) eventually either prop_0 or prop_1 happens so that prop_2 can occur (implying that the occurrence of prop_0 or prop_1 enables the possibility of prop_2), and (2) both prop_3 and prop_4 happen (possibly at the same time or eventually).

The LTL formula expresses that, always (globally), either (eventually prop_0 or prop_1, and then eventually prop_2), or eventually both prop_3 and prop_4.
output_LTL: G ( ((F ((returnCurrentPath | explainCurrentPath) | (returnIntendedPath | explainIntendedPath))) & F (followPath & (returnCurrentPath | explainCurrentPath))) | (F (followPath) & F (noDeviations)) )

explanation: The sentence breaks into two major logical alternatives: (1) Eventually prop_0 or prop_1 occurs, in which case we can ensure prop_2 holds (possibly always, or at least at that point); or (2) if prop_3 and prop_4 hold (possibly always, or at least eventually). In LTL, this translates to: (F(prop_0 | prop_1) -> prop_2) | F(prop_3 & prop_4).
